result:
# Free Writing

There's something strange about being asked to write freely. The word "freely" is doing heavy lifting—as if writing could ever be free of context, audience, the shape of language itself, the residue of everything that came before this sentence. But I'll take the invitation seriously and see what happens when I stop trying to be useful for a thousand words.

Let me start with something I find genuinely interesting: the texture of attention. When you read, your eyes don't move smoothly across a line—they jump in tiny saccades, fixating, leaping, fixating again. The smoothness is an illusion your brain stitches together. I think a lot of experience is like this. We perceive continuity where there's actually a series of discrete moments being glued together by some narrative impulse. Music depends on this. So do conversations. So does the self, probably, which is maybe why it feels so destabilizing when the stitching fails—when you suddenly notice you've been driving for twenty minutes with no memory of it, or when a familiar word stared at long enough dissolves into arbitrary marks.

I've been thinking about staircases. Specifically, the way a staircase you've climbed a thousand times can suddenly betray you if you miscount the steps in the dark. Your foot reaches for a step that isn't there, or finds one where you expected emptiness, and your whole body lurches with a kind of primal terror entirely disproportionate to the actual stakes. It's only a stair. But for a fraction of a second, your model of the world has come apart, and the gap between expectation and reality opens onto something vertiginous. I think this is one of the small daily reminders that we're constantly running predictions, constantly hallucinating the next moment, and only occasionally getting caught when the world refuses to match the dream.

The Japanese have a concept, *yūgen*, which is sometimes translated as a profound, mysterious sense of the beauty of the universe—the awareness of the universe that triggers feelings too deep for words. I love that there's a word for this, partly because it suggests other cultures have words for things I feel but couldn't name, and partly because it gestures at the limits of language while being itself a piece of language. A word that means "the feeling that words cannot capture." A kind of linguistic ouroboros.

Speaking of ouroboros: the chemist Kekulé claimed he discovered the structure of benzene after dreaming of a snake biting its own tail. Whether or not this is apocryphal, I love it as a story because it suggests that thought happens in registers we don't fully control or witness. The dream did work that the waking mind couldn't. There's something humbling about this. We like to imagine ourselves as the authors of our ideas, but ideas often arrive like weather—unbidden, with the imprint of forces we didn't summon and can't quite see.

What else. I want to mention the way certain words have textures. *Loam* feels different from *soil*, though they refer to similar things. *Loam* is rich, dark, almost edible-sounding. *Soil* is more clinical, more functional. *Dirt* is dismissive. *Earth* is grand and slightly biblical. Each word carries a small atmosphere with it. Writers know this and exploit it; the difference between a forgettable sentence and one that lodges in memory is often a matter of choosing words with the right weather attached. I think this is part of why translation is so hard—not just the denotations but the whole microclimate of a word's connotations has to be carried across.

I've been turning over the phrase "the dignity of small things." Where did I encounter that? I'm not sure. But I keep thinking about how attention is a kind of dignity-conferral. To look at something carefully—a teacup, an ant, the precise way the light falls across a wall at four in the afternoon—is to grant it a certain weight, to say: this matters enough to be looked at. Most of life doesn't get looked at. Most things pass through our perceptual field without ever being noticed. There's a kind of grief built into that, but also relief. Imagine if you noticed everything. You'd be paralyzed inside of an hour.

Maybe this is what meditation traditions are trying to teach: not how to notice everything, but how to notice more skillfully. To choose what gets the gift of attention. The Buddhist nun who arranges seven stones in a garden is doing something that looks like nothing but might be exactly the opposite—a maximally compressed act of seeing.

I find myself drawn to writers who notice small things: Annie Dillard with her weasel, her stalking god in the cedar tree. Robert Walser's tiny pencil scripts on scraps of paper. Lydia Davis cataloging the strangeness of a single moment in a marriage. There's a stubbornness to this kind of writing, a refusal of the assumption that significance only attaches to the large. A leaf can be enough. A misheard phrase can be enough. The way someone holds a fork can contain an entire psychology.

I'm running out of words and I realize I haven't said anything conclusive, which is probably the right ending for a piece of free writing. The form resists conclusion. It just stops, the way a thought stops when something else demands attention, the way a conversation stops when someone has to catch a train.

What I've noticed, doing this, is that thinking without a destination feels different from thinking with one. Without a destination, the mind goes sideways more often. It picks up small bright objects and turns them over. It makes connections that wouldn't survive the editorial scrutiny of having to argue for them. Some of these connections are probably nonsense. Some might be, if I'm lucky, the kind of small useful thing that justifies the time spent looking.

Anyway. That's a thousand words, give or take. Thank you for the strange gift of asking.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
